197. There's a pretty cool example. You can spend some time with it on your own, but it has to do with the selling of the little Harvard Classics books, and kind of a happy accident that occurred that then changed the way they advertised the books for evermore, tying in with aspirations. 199. There's a chapter out of Peterman's book about his catalog business. The title of the chapter is the Catalog that started a cult. And there's a couple of things I would highlight for you about this. One is on page 200 in a review of the catalog in a New York Times Sunday magazine, they describe Peterman as a merchant poet. Probably nobody's ever been described that before in the catalog industry, but it's actually a pretty good explanation of what goes on with the Peterman catalog. What's even more telling is on the next page, this customer saying, knowing about the catalog was sort of an inside thing. It was like being a member of a club. And so again, Peterman has accomplished the objective of a door people walk through, a unique place they feel they are in on the other side, and the copy largely appeals to aspirations rather than anything else. And so if you study a Peterman catalog, you'll find that the blue blazer is not being sold as a blue blazer either based on its features or its benefits. It is being sold about being David Niven, Kerry Grant-like, cool and unflappable and glamorous. And the copy is all about the guy walking out of a bomb crater with smoke heaven gone off and the jacket being perfectly pressed into the handkerchief matching the tie. So buying the jacket, you're buying wanting to be that guy, and the way you get to be the guy is putting on the jacket, and that's how you sell a $99 jacket for $499. It's not about the jacket. Peterman's a fabulous example of everything we're talking about. Principle 29, which is on page 204, the principle of the big entrance. Every piece of writing with rare exception, newsletter, book, article, e-mail, fax, in your continuous communication with your crowd is best served by your big entrance. We sometimes take the classic steps, attention, interest, desire. We sometimes take the attention of the people with whom we communicate all the time for granted that they will give us attention because of who we are and our relationship with them. And it's a bad presumption. It was not a bad presumption a decade ago when there weren't as many of us showing up every day asking for their attention. But now there's a lot more of us showing up every day asking for their attention, and so we got to earn it every single time. And so the whole issue of how you start matters a lot. So I've given you some examples. Here's some examples from fiction on page 204 of opening sentences. I particularly like the Mary had loved the family acts as a glittering extension of her own arm. I mean, you kind of like got to know where that goes, don't you? I mean, there's some more on page 205. On page 206 are some nonfiction openings. I would take this opportunity though to again encourage those of you who don't read fiction to start adding fiction to your diet. So here's some nonfiction openings. And Tom Peters one's pretty esoteric. On page 208 I took the nonfiction openings and kind of gave you the response that I thought they evoked from the reader. Because when you create your opening, you want to think about what response you want to evoke from the reader. And these examples, what this does, you know my headline test for advertising, right? Everybody know my headline test? What? Where have you been all these years, my friend? This is Kennedy 101. Good for you. I would hope so. You take the headline away from everything else, run it as a classified ad with only a response instruction, will anybody respond? I can do most of them. Harrison can do the 12 Commandments. So the same thing with opening lines and opening paragraphs and writing is if you take it away from everything else. Because when you write, you tend to write it like continuously. You don't think about it as isolated component parts. So if you take your opening sentence or your opening paragraph away from everything else and they only read that and it was on a page by itself and they couldn't get to anything else right away. I mean what response does it evoke from them and what response do you want it to evoke from them? So you pick it. You don't just kind of sloppily do it. And so here's a little exercise I would like you to do. Now take your stuff. Go to the tables with your samples that you brought that I copied. And in a few cases like John now, your catalog if you would pass it out to everybody. Or take it back and plop it on the table and let them get it. Bill, you brought I think a pile of things. Yeah, so anybody that has a pile of things, distribute your pile. Now let's take a look at your stuff that you brought isolating the openings. And see what you got. Does it hold up? What response does the first sentence, the first paragraph, the very short opening of the piece, the opening of each page? What response does it evoke? What response did you want it to evoke? How might it be made better? So sort of take a look at your own stuff and see how good of a job that you did. And see if somebody can spot where they are really either consistently doing something great, consistently missing the boat altogether. You got a really good example you want to share when we come back together. So please divide up, take a look at your own stuff. Again, help each other with your own stuff. And let's take 15 minutes.
